steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks for the main idea of the text. The text establishes that the sonnet has a reputation for rigidity, then argues (via Carl Phillips) that it actually invites experimentation and variety.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘Although Anderson's and Jess's sonnets are both widely celebrated for their striking originality, most modern examples of the form are generally regarded as conventional.’: The text says the opposite — it highlights experimental sonnets and argues the form invites variety. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘As a form, the sonnet encourages a surprising amount of variety, even though certain characteristics associated with it suggest this would be unlikely.’: Matches exactly: the sonnet's rigid reputation is contrasted with its actual capacity for experimentation. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘That the sonnet remains as popular as it is today is unexpected, given that many of the features associated with the form have long since seemed antiquated to readers.’: The text is about variety and experimentation, not about the sonnet's continued popularity. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘Although the sonnet is now recognized for the way it facilitates experimentation, there was a long period in its history in which very little innovation occurred.’: The text makes no historical claim about a period of stagnation. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
